our_argument_summary: Our review says WIN-042 repeats WIN-037 with identical threshold (>=28 nT) and identical NOAA/INTERMAGNET data sources. Regional SAA decay of 20–35 nT/yr is real but local, while global dipole decay is ~15 nT/yr. Duplicating the same claim across two WIN IDs does not create two independent confirmations. Verdict: Misleading.

relevant_files: raw-text/05-model.txt — 'Field decay rate: >=28 nT/year' listed as core model parameter (line 42) and 'Field Decay Rate: >=28 nT/year (2000-2025), projected 2030: -32 nT/year' (line 75)
raw-text/02-wins.txt — V50.6 only goes to WIN-039; WIN-042 does not appear in the extracted raw text (added in V51.0)
raw-text/03-predictions.txt — PRED-012: 'Field Decay >=28 nT/year by 2030 (PENDING)' — appears to be the long-term version of the same prediction
monitor.py — not available as extracted file; from WIN-004/005/037 curmudgeon reviews, Domain 8 ('SAA Decay Rate TTB') monitors Tsumeb station for >=28 nT/yr
inject_ai_layer.py — referenced in prior reviews as containing static WIN metadata; not directly available for inspection
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-042 has no dedicated monitoring domain. It shares the same >=28 nT/yr threshold as WIN-037 (PROS-007, W011) and the same INTERMAGNET/NOAA data sources. The dome's monitoring infrastructure (per cross-referenced reviews) only checks Tsumeb (TTB) via Domain 8 against this hardcoded threshold. WIN-042 was added in V51.0 as an apparently distinct WIN from WIN-037, but the threshold, the data sources, and the monitoring infrastructure are all identical. The only textual difference is framing: WIN-037 is 'Field Decay >=28 nT Since March 2025' (specific time window), WIN-042 is 'Field decay >=28 nT/year' (ongoing rate). Both reduce to 'SAA region exceeds 28 nT/yr decline, as it has for over two decades.'
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Same hardcoded threshold as WIN-037. Domain 8 compares Tsumeb station decay rate against a static >=28 nT/yr threshold. No code dynamically computes a global field average. No separate domain or computation handles WIN-042 distinctly from WIN-037. The distinction between 'cumulative since March 2025' and 'annual rate' is semantically trivial — the same single-station observation satisfies both framings.
data_sources_used: INTERMAGNET Tsumeb (TTB) station (live data via monitor.py Domain 8, shared with WIN-037)
NOAA WMM2025 (claimed confirmation source, shared with WIN-037)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: WIN-042 inherits WIN-037's escape clauses. The prediction says 'global magnetic field' but is confirmed using SAA-only regional data. The >=28 nT/yr threshold is set well below the SAA core decay rate (~30 nT/yr), virtually guaranteeing confirmation. Additionally, by framing WIN-042 as the 'rate' version and WIN-037 as the 'cumulative' version of the same observation, the dome creates a guaranteed-confirmation pair: both will always pass or fail together, since they test the same underlying measurement.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Minor: We cannot verify WIN-042's exact wording on the dome site because it was added in V51.0 and the V51.0 raw text has not been extracted. Our detail_claim ('Global magnetic field decays at rate ≥28 nT/year') may or may not match the dome's V51.0 phrasing exactly. However, the dome model's parameter table (raw-text/05-model.txt line 42) lists 'Field decay rate: >=28 nT/year' and PRED-012 mentions 'Field Decay >=28 nT/year by 2030,' so our characterization is consistent with available dome text.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-042 was added in V51.0 and does not appear in the V50.6 raw text. No monitoring domain in monitor.py handles 'field decay rate' as a distinct check separate from Domain 8. Domain 8 ('SAA Decay Rate TTB') monitors the Tsumeb station against >=28 nT/yr — this is the closest domain but is mapped to WIN-037 (via W011/PROS-007), not WIN-042. No additional domain, script, or automated check validates WIN-042 independently. Tagging as 'none' because WIN-042 has no dedicated monitoring infrastructure — it free-rides on Domain 8's WIN-037 check.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Geomagnetic field decay is continuously monitored by NOAA/NCEI (WMM series since 1965), ESA/Swarm (since 2013), IAGA (IGRF series since 1900), and INTERMAGNET (since 1991). Global dipole moment decline of ~5% per century is documented in Finlay et al. (2010), Thébault et al. (2015, IGRF-12), and Alken et al. (2021, IGRF-13). SAA regional decay rates of 20-35 nT/yr are tracked in WMM technical reports and research literature (Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016, Terra-Nova et al. 2017). The dome model relabels the cause as 'aetheric field decay' instead of 'outer core convection and reversed-flux patch growth' but predicts no different numerical value.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: WIN-042 was added in V51.0 (April 2026). The SAA core has been declining at 25-35 nT/yr continuously since at least 2000, documented in published literature throughout that period. The 28 nT/yr threshold matches the lower bound of observed SAA core decay rates and is listed as a model PARAMETER (raw-text/05-model.txt line 42: 'Field decay rate: >=28 nT/year'), meaning it was adopted from observation. The parameter predates WIN-042, which retroactively promotes an existing model input as a confirmed 'prediction.' Even WIN-037 (the earlier version of this same claim, registered March 2026) was confirmed from data already available at registration.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The 28 nT/yr value appears in the dome's parameter table but no derivation connects it to dome geometry. No formula starts from disc_radius (20,015 km), firmament_height (9,086 km), or any other dome geometric parameter and arrives at 28 nT/yr. The dome's field equation H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) describes spatial variation of the firmament height, not temporal field decay rates. The value is observationally adopted from INTERMAGNET station trends. PRED-012 projects 32 nT/yr by 2030 — also stated without geometric derivation.

holes_found.description: Our review's detail_evidence is thin — only three sentences. For a WIN that we claim is a duplicate of WIN-037, we should explicitly identify what distinguishes the two (if anything) and why the distinction fails. Currently, we assert 'WIN-042 repeats WIN-037' without showing the reader what WIN-037 claims vs. what WIN-042 claims, and why they collapse to the same test. A dome defender would say: 'WIN-037 is about cumulative decay since a specific date (March 2025), WIN-042 is about the ongoing annual rate being >=28 nT/yr — these are different measurable claims.'
holes_found.recommendation: Expand detail_evidence to address the semantic distinction: 'WIN-037 (PROS-007) claims the global field decayed by >=28 nT in the 12 months since March 2025. WIN-042 claims the ongoing decay rate is >=28 nT/year. These are mathematically equivalent: if the field dropped >=28 nT in any 12-month window, then the annual rate was >=28 nT/yr during that window. The same NOAA/INTERMAGNET data confirms both simultaneously — passing one guarantee passes the other. This is a single observation counted twice.'

holes_found.description: Our detail_claim says 'Global magnetic field decays at rate ≥28 nT/year' but we haven't verified this against the dome's actual V51.0 text for WIN-042. The V50.6 raw text stops at WIN-039. The dome's model parameter table says 'Field decay rate: >=28 nT/year' and PRED-012 says 'Field Decay >=28 nT/year by 2030' — but we don't know if the dome frames WIN-042 as 'global' or 'SAA-specific' or something else. If the dome site frames WIN-042 as specifically about the SAA rate rather than the global rate, our 'global vs. local conflation' argument from WIN-037 may not apply here.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) extract V51.0 raw text to verify WIN-042's exact wording, or (b) adjust our argument to focus on the duplication aspect rather than the global/local conflation, since the duplication argument holds regardless of how WIN-042 is framed: 'Whether framed as global or regional, the same INTERMAGNET data and same >=28 nT/yr threshold that confirm WIN-037 also confirm WIN-042. No new measurement or test distinguishes them.'

holes_found.description: Our review claims 'global dipole decay is ~15 nT/yr' without citation. This was flagged as a major issue in the WIN-037 curmudgeon review. The problem persists in WIN-042's text — identical phrasing, identical lack of citation. Surface field intensity decline is location-dependent and the '~15 nT/yr' figure is imprecise. A dome defender could challenge: 'There is no single global nT/yr number; the dome's threshold of 28 nT/yr is scientifically reasonable for the SAA region.'
holes_found.recommendation: Replace '~15 nT/yr' with a more defensible formulation. Either cite the specific source (e.g., 'global dipole moment is declining at ~5% per century, approximately 15-20 nT/yr in average surface intensity — Thébault et al. 2015, IGRF-12 technical note') or reframe: 'The >=28 nT/yr rate is met in the SAA core but not globally. Average surface intensity decline varies by location; much of the Northern Hemisphere is experiencing stable or increasing field intensity, contradicting the dome's implied global trend.'

holes_found.description: Our detail_evidence uses generic links: NOAA homepage (https://www.noaa.gov/) and INTERMAGNET homepage (https://www.intermagnet.org/). Neither link leads to the specific field decay data referenced. This was also flagged for WIN-037. Identical generic citations in two adjacent WINs reinforce the impression of thin sourcing.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace with specific links: NOAA WMM product page (https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/world-magnetic-model), INTERMAGNET data access portal (https://www.intermagnet.org/data-donnee/download-eng.php), or specific IGRF technical report URLs.

holes_found.description: The detail_group 'WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063' correctly clusters WIN-042 with related magnetic field WINs. However, our detail_evidence doesn't reference this grouping or explain why these 8 WINs form a cluster. The grouping exists in the metadata but is invisible to the reader in the detail view.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider adding to detail_extra a sentence acknowledging the cluster: 'WIN-042 is part of a group of 8 V51.0 additions (WIN-040–043, 059–061, 063) that reslice previously confirmed geomagnetic observations into separate WIN IDs. This pattern of WIN count inflation is analyzed in Part 4.6.'

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's own parameter table lists 'Field decay rate: >=28 nT/year' as a model INPUT, not a derived prediction. WIN-042 claims this rate as a confirmed prediction, but confirmed against what? The model was configured with this value from observation. This is circular: the model says the field decays at >=28 nT/yr because the model was built with that number. This is the same structural problem identified in WIN-037 (where the WIN-037 review's 'stronger_arguments[3]' made this point), and it applies identically to WIN-042.
sources: raw-text/05-model.txt line 75: 'Field Decay Rate: >=28 nT/year (2000-2025), projected 2030: -32 nT/year'

stronger_arguments.description: WIN-042 and WIN-037 form a guaranteed-confirmation pair. Both use the same threshold (>=28 nT), the same data sources (NOAA/INTERMAGNET), and the same underlying measurement (SAA regional decay rate). They are mathematically equivalent: if the field dropped >=28 nT cumulatively (WIN-037), then the annualized rate is >=28 nT/yr (WIN-042). No possible measurement outcome could confirm one and falsify the other. In any legitimate prediction registry, these would be one entry, not two. The dome counts them as two separate WINs, inflating the confirmed count from 66 to 67 for zero additional evidential content.
sources: raw-text/03-predictions.txt line 44: PRED-012 ongoing rate prediction

stronger_arguments.description: PRED-012 ('Field Decay >=28 nT/year by 2030') is still listed as PENDING in the predictions file, yet WIN-042 claims the same rate is already CONFIRMED. This is internally inconsistent: either the >=28 nT/yr rate is confirmed (making PRED-012 redundant) or it's still pending (making WIN-042 premature). The dome appears to be double-dipping — claiming credit for the confirmation now (WIN-042) while keeping the prediction open for future credit (PRED-012 'by 2030').
sources: raw-text/03-predictions.txt line 44: 'PRED-012 — Field Decay >=28 nT/year by 2030 (PENDING)'

kernel_of_truth.description: The geomagnetic field IS declining, and the rate of decline in the SAA core region does exceed 28 nT/yr. This is a real, measurable, and scientifically significant phenomenon. The dome model correctly highlights that Earth's magnetic field is weakening and that this ongoing trend has practical implications (satellite operations, radiation exposure). Monitoring field decay rates at INTERMAGNET stations is legitimate scientific practice, and the >=28 nT/yr figure is in the right ballpark for SAA-region stations.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Recognizing an ongoing, well-documented trend is not predicting it. The SAA decay rate has been measured at 25-35 nT/yr for over two decades. Setting a threshold at the lower bound of the observed range guarantees confirmation — it's like predicting the sun will rise tomorrow. More importantly, WIN-042 adds nothing that WIN-037 doesn't already claim. The dome model provides no derivation of why the rate is ~28 nT/yr (rather than 10 or 50), no mechanism connecting dome geometry to temporal field evolution, and no prediction that distinguishes the dome model from standard geomagnetic models (WMM, IGRF, CHAOS-7) that all predict the same ongoing SAA decline. Counting the same trivially-confirmed observation under two WIN IDs does not produce two independent confirmations.

deeper_analysis:
WIN-042 is the clearest example of WIN count inflation in the V51.0 batch additions. The claim — field decay >=28 nT/yr — is mathematically equivalent to WIN-037 (field decay >=28 nT since March 2025). Both test the same measurement with the same threshold against the same data.

**The duplication mechanics:**
WIN-037 (PROS-007) was registered 2026-03-06 as a prospective prediction: 'The global geomagnetic field would have decayed by at least 28 nT in the 12 months since March 2025.' It was confirmed within 6 days using already-available INTERMAGNET data. WIN-042 appeared in V51.0 (April 2026) as an apparently distinct claim about the 'rate' being >=28 nT/yr. But a cumulative decline of >=28 nT over 12 months IS a rate of >=28 nT/yr. There is no measurement that could distinguish them.

**The PRED-012 inconsistency:**
Meanwhile, PRED-012 ('Field Decay >=28 nT/year by 2030') remains PENDING in the predictions file. This creates a logical paradox: the same assertion is simultaneously CONFIRMED (WIN-042) and PENDING (PRED-012). The 'by 2030' qualifier appears to mean 'the rate will still be >=28 nT/yr in 2030,' but WIN-042 already confirms the current rate meets this threshold. The dome appears to be holding PRED-012 in reserve for future WIN creation — another instance of the self-confirming prediction pipeline.

**The parameter-as-prediction circularity:**
The dome's model page lists 'Field decay rate: >=28 nT/year' as a core parameter. This means the 28 nT/yr value was adopted from observation into the model architecture. WIN-042 then claims the model 'predicted' this rate, but the model's 'prediction' is just reading back its own input. This circularity is pervasive across the V51.0 batch additions and is analyzed systematically in Part 4.6 of our review.

**Our review's weaknesses:**
The detail_evidence for WIN-042 is too terse (three sentences). It correctly identifies the duplication but doesn't show the reader WHY the two WINs collapse to the same test. The '~15 nT/yr' global figure (inherited from WIN-037's text) remains uncited — this was flagged as a major issue in the WIN-037 curmudgeon review and should be fixed in both WINs simultaneously. Generic NOAA/INTERMAGNET links should be replaced with specific data product URLs.

**Verdict assessment:**
'Misleading' is correct and well-supported. WIN-042 combines three 'Misleading' subcategories: (1) duplication of WIN-037, (2) circular parameter-as-prediction, and (3) trivially unfalsifiable threshold set within the long-established SAA trend. The verdict is solid; the supporting text just needs minor expansion to make the duplication argument explicit rather than merely asserted.

citations_verified: NOAA link (https://www.noaa.gov/) — resolves to NOAA homepage. Generic but valid.
INTERMAGNET link (https://www.intermagnet.org/) — resolves to INTERMAGNET homepage. Generic but valid.
citations_unchecked: '~15 nT/yr' global decay rate — no specific citation in our text. Should reference IGRF-13 (Alken et al. 2021, Earth Planets Space 73:49) or NOAA WMM2025 technical report for the global dipole decline rate.

'20–35 nT/yr' SAA regional rate — no specific citation. Plausible range from INTERMAGNET SAA station data; should cite Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis (2016, Earth-Science Reviews 154:96-125) or WMM2025 technical reports.
